# Multi-Perspective Validation

## Explains

Explains why decisions improve when subjected to multiple independent viewpoints before commitment.

## The Concept

No single perspective captures reality. Every observer has blind spots determined by their position, experience, and incentives. Validated decisions come from deliberately seeking disagreement, not consensus. The goal is not to find the perspective that is "right" but to triangulate across multiple perspectives to reveal the shape of the actual problem.

Techniques for multi-perspective validation include: red team/blue team exercises, multi-model consultation (asking different AI models the same question), cross-domain review (having someone from outside the domain examine the reasoning), adversarial testing (actively trying to break the conclusion), and pre-mortem analysis (imagining failure and working backward). Each technique provides a different angle on the same decision.

The framework's lens model is itself an application of this concept — the same governance structure viewed through different perspectives (structural, operational, strategic) reveals different truths. Quality of decisions correlates with diversity of input, not authority of decider. A CEO making a decision alone produces worse outcomes than a junior analyst whose conclusion has survived challenge from three independent perspectives.

## Why This Matters for Governance

Governance decisions — which rules to create, which standards to enforce, which objects to retire — are high-leverage choices that affect many people over long periods. They deserve multi-perspective validation more than most decisions. A governance rule created from a single perspective (e.g., "compliance says we need this") without challenge from other perspectives (operations, engineering, strategy) will likely produce unintended consequences.

JOS embeds this principle in its review processes and panel consultations. The standing order to seek multiple viewpoints before committing to governance changes is not bureaucracy — it is epistemological hygiene.

## Connects To

- JOS-C09 (Cynefin) — different Cynefin domains require different validation approaches
- JOS-P06 (Intelligence Preservation) — preserving the reasoning behind decisions enables future re-validation
- Framework §2 (lenses) — the lens model is multi-perspective validation applied to governance structure

## Provenance

Foundry "Multi-Perspective Validation" operations concept, reframed as a universal decision-quality principle. Roots in epistemology, intelligence analysis (Heuer's structured analytic techniques), and ensemble methods in machine learning.
